evidence_quotes.quote: shutdown of purchased digital products, including games and other types of software.

paragraphs.text: Hello, I support the goals of the Digital Fairness Act, especially its efforts to address dark patterns, misleading influencer marketing, addictive design, and unfair personalisation. However, the proposal overlooks a serious and growing issue: the shutdown of purchased digital products, including games and other types of software. The Stop Destroying Videogames initiative through the European Citizens' Initiative has gathered over 1.4 million signatures across the EU, showing just how widespread public concern is. Consumers are losing access to games and other products they paid for, not because the content no longer works, but because companies choose to disable or remove them. This often happens without refund, no option for users to preserve or continue using what they purchased. Access to games should not be removed, and consumers should retain access to what they purchase even after support ends. Ending support is not a valid reason to take away access. Shutting down purchased content cannot be justified when the product still functions and was sold without a clear expiry. To ensure fairness and accountability, games and other digital products should be required to have a clear end-of-life plan. This should include details on how access will be preserved, whether offline functionality will be provided, and how users will be informed and supported. I urge the Commission to strengthen the Digital Fairness Act by including: - Guaranteed access to purchased content, or compensation if access is removed. - A ban on removing access post-purchase, especially for products that still function. - Mandatory preservation options, such as offline modes or user-hosted servers. - Transparent, upfront communication, including end-of-life plans and support timelines. This is about more than games its about protecting consumers from losing access to digital products they paid for in good faith. Over 1.4 million people in the EU have spoken through the Stop Killing Games campaign. Please ensure their voices are heard. Thank you.
